text: The surgeon stands on the patient's right side, using laparoscopic instruments to take down the greater curvature vessels up to the angle of His and the left crus. The duodenum is visible above the pancreas below, with careful dissection around the gastroduodenal artery (GDA) for transection. The surgeon meticulously manages small bleeding vessels and avoids injury to surrounding structures such as the pancreas, GDA, and common bile duct.

The surgeon carefully dissects and takes down more vessels, creating a visible window on the opposite side. The pylorus is identified, and the duodenum is dissected with caution due to the proximity of the portal triad or common bile duct. The sleeve is tailored using a fifty French bougie, ensuring it does not twist. The surgeon identifies the ileocecal valve and measures the bowel to mark the 125-centimeter common channel, pointing to the pelvic side of the intestine.

The surgeon marks the common channel at 125 centimeters and the Roux limb at 150 centimeters. Using a white load stapler, the bowel is divided, followed by division of the mesentery. The mesenteric defect is closed with an Ethibond suture, aiming to connect the BPLM to the suture placed at the common channel area. The bowel is aligned, and the Roux limb is attached to the BPLM, completing the ileostomy.

The surgeon closes the ileoileostomy using a Vicryl suture, ensuring the mucosa is properly dunked and there is no narrowing. The silk suture is used to align the bowel, and an anti-obstruction stitch is attached to the Ethibond suture.

The surgeon performs a vertical transection of the duodenum using a stapler with a purple load through the supraumbilical port, ensuring the common bile duct is not grasped. The right gastric artery is typically sacrificed during this procedure. The posterior outer layer is sutured with a vascular suture, and the rulem is visible.

The surgeon performs a two-layered anastomosis on the Roux limb, starting with the posterior inner layer using vascular sutures. The enterotomy on the Roux limb is visibly wider than the duodenotomy, which is corrected with additional sutures. The surgeon continues with the anterior inner layer, ensuring proper alignment and closure. The anterior outer layer is then sutured and tied to the posterior outer layer. A scope is inserted into the Roux limb to test the anastomosis for leaks while clamping the Roux limb, completing the surgery.
